section.title: Diotrephes Rebuked
section.sectionRationale: The letter's dark centre: a church leader named Diotrephes, who loves to be first, rejects the elder's authority, slanders him, refuses the travelling brothers, and expels those who would welcome them. The elder answers with a plain principle: do not imitate evil but good.
section.thesis: Self-exalting leadership that loves preeminence stands opposed to the truth, and the way forward is not to copy such evil but to imitate what is good, for the one who does good is of God.
section.frameNote: Two beats: the charge against Diotrephes, who loves to be first; and the elder's appeal to imitate good rather than evil.

units.label: The charge against Diotrephes
units.note: a man who loves to be first

verses.text: I have written to the church about this, but Diotrephes, who {{philoproteuo|loves to be first}}, will not accept our instruction.

So if I come, I will call attention to his {{phlyareo|malicious slander}} against us. And unsatisfied with that, he refuses to welcome the brothers and forbids those who want to do so, even putting them out of the church.

units.label: The appeal to imitate good
units.note: the one who does good is of God

verses.text: Beloved, do not {{mimeomai|imitate}} what is evil, but what is good. The one who {{agathopoieo|does good}} is of God; the one who {{kakopoieo|does evil}} has not seen God.

philoproteuo.gloss: To love to be first, to crave preeminence and the top place. It appears only here in the New Testament, coined to expose Diotrephes exactly. His root sin is not false doctrine but a hunger for personal supremacy that poisons everything he does in the church.

phlyareo.gloss: To talk nonsense against, to bring unjustified and malicious charges. It pictures empty, wicked words used as weapons. Diotrephes did not refute the elder with truth; he tore at him with baseless slander, the tool of a man defending his own position.

mimeomai.gloss: To imitate, to copy as a pattern; the root of the word mimic. Discipleship works by imitation, so the elder tells Gaius which pattern to follow: not the evil of Diotrephes but the good, which ultimately means following God and those who reflect Him.

agathopoieo.gloss: To do good, to act rightly and beneficially. The one who habitually does good shows that he belongs to God, is of God, sharing His character. Doing good here is the visible evidence of a life truly connected to God.

kakopoieo.gloss: To do evil, to act wickedly. The one whose settled practice is evil, like Diotrephes, shows he has not seen God, has no real knowledge of or fellowship with Him. Conduct reveals the heart: what we habitually do exposes whose we are.

basic.context: Now the tone darkens. A man named Diotrephes had taken charge in a church and loved being first. He rejected the elder's authority, spread malicious lies about him, refused to welcome the travelling gospel workers, and even threw out members who did welcome them. The elder says he will deal with this if he comes, and then gives Gaius a simple rule: do not copy evil, copy good.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes a real and ugly church conflict; it does not commend Diotrephes. The lasting lessons are sharp: leadership that craves being first is a serious danger, slander and shutting people out are marks of a heart far from God, and the test of anyone's claim to know God is whether they actually do good.
basic.originalLanguages: Diotrephes is defined by a rare word, philoproteuo, loves to be first. He attacks the elder with phlyareo, malicious empty talk, slander. The elder tells Gaius to mimeomai, imitate, not evil but good. The one who does good, agathopoieo, is of God; the one who does evil, kakopoieo, has not seen God.
basic.typology: Diotrephes, grasping to be first, is the opposite of Christ, who did not grasp at status but made Himself nothing and took the lowest place to serve. He is a warning picture of the self-exalting leader, set against Jesus, the servant King who is truly first because He humbled Himself.
basic.christ: Jesus taught that whoever wants to be first must be the servant of all, and He lived it, washing His disciples' feet and dying for them. Diotrephes is everything Christ is not. To imitate what is good is to imitate Christ, the one who did good and is of God in the fullest sense.
basic.god: God is dishonoured by proud, domineering leadership that loves the top place and shuts others out. He is honoured by humble service and by doing good. And He sees the heart: our habits of good or evil reveal whether we truly know Him.
basic.crossReferences: Jesus says the greatest must be the servant. Paul says Christ did not grasp at equality but humbled Himself. And Peter warns elders not to lord it over the flock but to be examples to it.
basic.oneStory: The Bible's story is the undoing of proud self-rule by the humble Servant King. Diotrephes shows the old grasping way still at work inside the church, while Christ shows the new way of lowly service. Gaius, and every believer, is called to imitate the good and so belong to God.

context.literary: The negative counterpart to Gaius, placed deliberately between the commendation of Gaius and Demetrius. The elder stacks the charges against Diotrephes, verses 9 and 10, then draws the moral in verse 11 with a general principle about good and evil that reaches beyond this one man to every reader.
context.historical: Diotrephes appears to be a prominent, perhaps self-appointed, leader in a local church who resisted the elder's apostolic oversight. His refusal to receive the travelling brothers, and his expelling of those who did, was an abuse of local power that cut the church off from the wider fellowship and from the elder himself. Such conflicts over authority and hospitality were real pressures in the young churches.
context.canonical: A stark New Testament case study in the abuse of church leadership, standing against the pattern of servant leadership taught by Jesus and the qualifications for elders in Peter and Paul. It shows that the gravest threats to a church can come not only from false teaching but from proud men within.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose and rebuke Diotrephes's self-exalting, divisive conduct, to assure the church that the elder will address it, and to fix in Gaius a governing principle: imitate good rather than evil, because conduct reveals whether one truly belongs to God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The account of Diotrephes is description and warning, not a model to follow. The prescriptive core is verse 11: do not imitate what is evil but what is good, with the test that doing good shows we are of God and doing evil shows we have not seen Him.

debates.question: Was Diotrephes a heretic or something else?

views.label: A power problem, not a doctrine problem
views.case: The text never charges him with false teaching; his sin is loving to be first, slandering the elder, and excluding others. The danger highlighted is pride and abuse of authority within an otherwise orthodox church.

views.label: Pride that endangers the truth
views.case: Even without stated heresy, his rejection of apostolic instruction and of gospel workers undermines the truth in practice, so his conduct is a spiritual threat, not a mere personality flaw.

debates.bearing: Either way the letter warns that self-exalting leadership is a grave danger in its own right, and that character and conduct, not just doctrine, reveal whether someone is of God.

study.originalLanguages: The rebuke is built on precise words. Diotrephes is branded by philoproteuo, a term found only here, loves to be first, naming a craving for preeminence as the root of his behaviour. He wields phlyareo against the elder, malicious, empty talk, slander rather than argument. Against this, the elder urges mimeomai, imitation, the ordinary engine of discipleship: copy the good, not the evil. And the test is conduct: the one who practises good, agathopoieo, is of God, while the one who practises evil, kakopoieo, has not seen God. What we habitually do reveals whose we are.

typology.fulfillment: The dark opposite of Christ, who did not grasp at status but emptied Himself and took the servant's place, Philippians 2:5-8.

typology.type: The one who does good is of God
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled and embodied in Jesus, who went about doing good and is of God in the fullest sense, the pattern His people imitate.

study.christ: Diotrephes is a portrait of everything Jesus refused to be. Where Diotrephes loved to be first, Jesus taught that whoever would be first must be servant of all, and then proved it by washing feet and going to the cross. Where Diotrephes shut people out, Christ welcomes sinners. To imitate what is good, then, is finally to imitate Christ, who did good, served the lowest, and is truly of God. He is the servant King whose humility exposes and undoes the pride of every Diotrephes.
study.god: God is dishonoured by leaders who love the chief seat, silence rivals with slander, and drive others away. He is honoured by humble, hospitable service. And God reads the heart through the life: settled habits of doing good mark those who are of Him, while a pattern of evil shows a person has not truly seen Him, however prominent his position.

crossReferences.note: Jesus forbids lording it over others; the great must serve, for the Son of Man came to serve and give His life.
Do nothing from selfish ambition; Christ did not grasp status but humbled Himself, the antithesis of Diotrephes.
Elders are to shepherd willingly, not domineering over the flock but being examples to it.
Doing good or evil reveals whether one is a child of God, the same test applied here in verse 11.

study.oneStory: The whole Bible traces the overthrow of proud, grasping self-rule by a humble Servant King. Diotrephes shows that old grasping spirit still at work even inside the church, loving to be first at the cost of others. Christ shows the new and better way, first because He made Himself last. Gaius, and every reader, is called to imitate the good and so prove to belong to the God who is undoing pride and remaking a humble people.

sermon.title: The Condemnation of Diotrephes
sermon.why: Begg devotes this message in his 3 John series to Diotrephes, showing how a bad ego left unchecked produces chaos in the church, and setting his self-exaltation against the call to imitate what is good. A clear, orthodox treatment of this movement.

quiz.q: What was the root problem with Diotrephes?

options.text: He loved to be first, craving preeminence and rejecting others' authority.
options.note: Verse 9 names his love of being first as the driving sin behind his behaviour.

options.text: He abused his authority, rejecting oversight and controlling the church.
options.note: True of his conduct, but Scripture roots it in his love of being first.
The text never charges him with false teaching; the issue is pride and power.

options.text: He was simply shy and withdrawn.
options.note: He was domineering and aggressive, not withdrawn.

quiz.q: What did Diotrephes do to those who wanted to welcome the brothers?

options.text: He forbade them and even put them out of the church.
options.note: Verse 10 records that he expelled those who welcomed the workers.

options.text: He refused to welcome the brothers and pressured others to do the same.
options.note: True, but the sharpest detail is that he expelled those who did welcome them.

options.text: He quietly disagreed but let them carry on.
options.note: He did not merely disagree; he actively forbade and expelled them.

options.text: He welcomed the brothers himself instead.
options.note: He refused to welcome them at all.

quiz.q: What principle does the elder give Gaius in verse 11?

options.text: Do not imitate evil but good, for the one who does good is of God.
options.note: Verse 11 sets the governing rule: copy good, not evil, as the mark of belonging to God.

options.text: Copy good examples rather than bad ones in the church.
options.note: Right as far as it goes, but it omits the test that doing good shows we are of God.

options.text: Avoid all church leaders from now on.
options.note: The point is discernment between good and evil, not withdrawal from the church.

options.text: Repay Diotrephes with the same slander.
options.note: The call is to imitate good, not to answer evil with evil.

quiz.q: How does Diotrephes contrast with Jesus?

options.text: Diotrephes grasped to be first; Jesus took the servant's place and made Himself last.
options.note: Christ, who served and washed feet, is the opposite of Diotrephes, Mark 10:45.

options.text: Jesus served others, while Diotrephes served only himself.
options.note: True and to the point, a simpler statement of the same contrast.

options.text: Both simply wanted to lead the church well.
options.note: Diotrephes wanted supremacy for himself; Jesus led by humble service.

options.text: Diotrephes is a good model of leadership to follow.
options.note: He is a warning, the very opposite of Christlike leadership.

questions: Diotrephes loved to be first. Where do you notice the desire to be first, to be seen, or to be in control creeping into your own heart?
He used slander to protect his position. When you feel threatened, do you reach for truth and humility, or for words that tear others down?
The elder tells Gaius to imitate good, not evil. Whose life are you actually copying, and is it drawing you toward Christ or away from Him?
Doing good is the evidence of truly knowing God. If your habits this month were the measure, what would they say about whose you are?
Jesus said the first must be the servant of all. Where could you deliberately take the lower place this week for the good of others?

translationNotes.note: I have written to the church renders a phrase the BSB footnote gives literally as I have written something to the church; the reference is likely a short letter now lost, or possibly 2 John. Loves to be first translates philoproteuo, a word found only here.
Malicious slander renders phlyareo with a modifier, literally talking wicked nonsense against; it pictures baseless, empty accusations used as weapons.
Has not seen God echoes John's usage where seeing God means true spiritual knowledge and fellowship, not physical sight; conduct reveals the reality of that relationship.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 3 John 1:9-11. The BSB footnote at verse 9 notes the literal I have written something to the church.
